coredata.dc:description: The increase in oxytetracycline (OTC) pollution has become a significant risk to ecological stability and human health because of excessive use. Therefore, developing a precise and reliable sensor for trace OTC detection is critical. In this work, a dual-mode sensor of colorimetric-fluorescent (CL-FL) with dual signal-on was developed for OTC detection. Firstly, a novel carbon dots encapsulating in cavities of MOFs composite (CDs@NH2-MIL-88B) was constructed by the ship-in-a-bottle approach, which enhanced peroxidase-like activity and fluorescent intensity. Further, combining with surface molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP), the dual-mode sensor was constructed to develop selectivity (CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP). Finally, the dual signal enhancement mechanism of the sensor comes from the interaction between OTC and identifying binding site involving strong hydrogen bonding and metal complexation from imprinted cavity and CDs@NH2-MIL-88B, respectively. The prepared sensor has a linear detection range of 1.00 × 10−9 - 1.00 × 10−5 M with the limit of detection (LOD) of 1.47 × 10−10 M for the CL mode and a detection range of 1.00 × 10−11 −1.00 × 10−7 M with the LOD of 1.84 × 10−12 M for the FL mode. Meanwhile, the sensor shows high sensitivity, accuracy and reliability in OTC detection. Overall, this work provides a promising CL-FL dual-mode sensing systems with signal-on in the detection of antibiotics and other hazardous pollutants.

head.ce:title: Anchor carbon dots inside NH2-MIL-88B via ship-in-a-bottle strategy for dual signal enhancement in colorimetric-fluorescent sensors

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The increase in oxytetracycline (OTC) pollution has become a significant risk to ecological stability and human health because of excessive use. Therefore, developing a precise and reliable sensor for trace OTC detection is critical. In this work, a dual-mode sensor of colorimetric-fluorescent (CL-FL) with dual signal-on was developed for OTC detection. Firstly, a novel carbon dots encapsulating in cavities of MOFs composite (CDs@NH2-MIL-88B) was constructed by the ship-in-a-bottle approach, which enhanced peroxidase-like activity and fluorescent intensity. Further, combining with surface molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP), the dual-mode sensor was constructed to develop selectivity (CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP). Finally, the dual signal enhancement mechanism of the sensor comes from the interaction between OTC and identifying binding site involving strong hydrogen bonding and metal complexation from imprinted cavity and CDs@NH2-MIL-88B, respectively. The prepared sensor has a linear detection range of 1.00 × 10−9 - 1.00 × 10−5 M with the limit of detection (LOD) of 1.47 × 10−10 M for the CL mode and a detection range of 1.00 × 10−11 −1.00 × 10−7 M with the LOD of 1.84 × 10−12 M for the FL mode. Meanwhile, the sensor shows high sensitivity, accuracy and reliability in OTC detection. Overall, this work provides a promising CL-FL dual-mode sensing systems with signal-on in the detection of antibiotics and other hazardous pollutants.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Oxytetracycline (OTC) is commonly used in aquaculture, medicine and stockbreeding due to its broad antimicrobial spectrum and cost-effectiveness. However, there has been a concerning trend of OTC being detected in various water environments in recent years, which threatens human health and ecological safety [1]. Various techniques have been developed to detect OTC, such as liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [2], high-performance liquid chromatography-ultraviolet spectrometry (HPLC-UV) [3], electrochemical (EC) [4], photoelectrochemical (PEC) [5], surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [6], fluorescent (FL) [7] and colorimetric (CL) [8]. Among them, optical methods such as FL and CL, have attracted much attention due to their visual, simple, efficient and cost-effectiveness. However, the accuracy of single signal method still needs to be improved under complex matrix interference [9]. Recently, dual-mode sensing has been widely concerned in different objects detection due to its advantages of self-calibration, such as CL-PEC[10], FL-PEC[11], photothermal-electrochemical (PT-EC)[12], fluorescence-electrochemiluminescence (FL-ECL)[13] and CL-FL [14,15]. Among them, the CL-FL dual-mode sensing strategy based on MIP (MIP CL-FL) has received widespread attention due to its advantages in high sensitivity, stability, accuracy and double visual analysis. However, most of them were signal-off, which may have high background signals and complex matrix interference [16,17]. The dual signal-on strategy can reduce false-positive results to provide more reliable results than the signal-off strategy and single mode detection [13]. Therefore, it is necessary to further develop CL-FL dual signal-on strategy for trace OTC detection.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) constructed from different organic ligands and metal ions have been widely used in CL and FL sensing due to their high enzyme-like activity and exceptional optical properties [18,19]. Among them, NH2-MIL-88B has strong peroxidase-like activity and blue fluorescence due to the iron (Fe) node and 2-aminoterephthalic acid (NH2-BDC) ligand respectively. Li et al. found the Fe node of NH2-MIL-88B could form metal complexes with OTC, enhancing the peroxidase-like activity of NH2-MIL-88B [20]. Wu et al. found UiO-66 with same ligand as NH2-MIL-88B could form strong hydrogen bonds with C = O and C-N groups on norfloxacin to enhance fluorescence [21]. Therefore, it is expected to achieve MIP CL-FL dual signal-on sensing based on the interaction of NH2-MIL-88B with the C = O and C-N groups of OTC. However, molecularly imprinted membranes on MOFs always reduce their peroxidase-like activity and fluorescence, decreasing the sensitivity of sensor. Therewith, the host-guest assembly strategy can used to improve material activity in MIP sensing due to adjustable pore size and ultra-high specific surface area of MOFs [22]. These guest species encapsulating in the cavities of MOFs are mainly quantum dots [23–25] and precious metal nanoparticles [26,27]. Among them, the CDs, as a new type of carbon nanomaterial, has been widely used in catalysis, sensing and bioimaging fields due to exceptional enzyme-like activity and fluorescence properties [28–30]. Incorporating CDs into MOFs can enhance electron transfer and improve the enzyme-like activity of MOFs [31]. Additionally, the pore structure of MOFs could prevent FL quenching caused by CDs self-aggregation, thus enhancing their FL properties [32]. However, the low loading of CDs in the composite are still a challenge [31]. Currently, Gu et al. successfully improved CDs loading in the pores of MOFs by loading glucose into MOFs and low-temperature calcination [33]. This innovative ship-in-a-bottle approach presents a new method for creating CDs/MOFs composites. Therefore, it is expected to develop highly sensitive and selective dual signal-on MIP CL-FL sensing based NH2-MIL-88B confined CDs by ship-in-a-bottle for OTC detection.

In this work, CDs were in situ anchored in the pore of NH2-MIL-88B by the ship-in-a-bottle strategy to obtain CDs@NH2-MIL-88B with excellent peroxidase-like activity and fluorescence properties. The MIP CL-FL dual-mode sensor with dual signal-on was developed combining it with molecularly imprinting technology (CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP) for OTC detection. The imprinted cavities can specifically capture OTC molecules, leading to an enhancement in both enzyme-like activity and fluorescence by electron transfer acceleration, increased affinity, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and restriction of intramolecularly motions (RIM) effects, which results in the dual signal-on generation of CL and FL ( Scheme 1). Based on variations in CL and FL signals induced by OTC concentration, the wide linear range, high sensitivity, accurate and reliable detection of OTC could be achieved using the CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP activated CL-FL dual-mode sensing method.
A comprehensive list of all chemicals and instruments are provided in the Supplementary Material.

CDs@NH2-MIL-88B was prepared using the ship-in-a-bottle strategy [28]. CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP was prepared based on references with some modifications [34]. The relevant synthesis details are provided in the Supplementary Material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CL-FL dual-mode sensor for OTC detection
ce:section.ce:para: The CL-FL dual-mode sensor for OTC detection was performed as follows: 100 μL OTC standard solutions with different concentrations and 100 μL 150 μg mL−1 CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP were added to different centrifuge tubes containing 600 μL NaAc-HAc buffer (pH 4.0, 0.1 M). After incubating for 15 min at room temperature, For FL model, the FL signal of samples was directly measured by the fluorescence spectrometry. For CL model, 100 μL 0.1 mM H2O2 and 100 μL 1 mM TMB were added to the above mixed solution and reacted for 10 min at room temperature. Subsequently, the UV–vis absorbance of the samples was recorded.

The actual samples were acquired from a river in the Dantu District of Zhenjiang, China. Before analysis, they were filtered through a 0.22 µm filter membrane to remove suspended solids and impurities. Then, spiked actual samples containing OTC were prepared using the standard addition method and detected by the developed CL-FL dual-mode sensor. Finally, the accuracy of the sensor was verified using LC-MS/MS standard method.

First, the morphology of NH2-MIL-88B and CDs@NH2-MIL-88B was characterised by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1A, NH2-MIL-88B has an octahedral structure with a particle size of 210 nm and no internal granular substances. The particle size of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B was slightly reduced to 170 nm, while obvious granular substances could be observed inside the composite (Fig. 1B). After compositing the molecularly imprinted layer, the particle size of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP increased significantly to about 240 nm, and it was challenging to observe the granular substances inside due to the formation of the molecularly imprinted membrane (Fig. 1C) [35]. HRTEM was performed to prove that these granular substances were CDs. As shown in Fig. 1D and E, the particle size was about 3 nm and the lattice spacing was 0.260 nm corresponded to the (020) characteristic lattice stripe of carbon, proving that CDs were successfully synthesised [36]. More dispersed CDs were also observed in CDs@NH2-MIL-88B with a lattice spacing of 0.261 nm consistent with the lattice spacing of CDs (Fig. 1F). The above results show that CDs@NH2-MIL-88B were successfully prepared by the ship-in-a-bottle strategy.

EDS characterisation was also performed to further demonstrate the successful preparation of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP. As shown in Fig. 1G, the CDs@NH2-88B@rMIP comprised Fe, Si, C, N and O elements. The uniformly distributed Si elements come from molecularly imprinting polymer. The above results demonstrate the successful synthesis of CDs@NH2-88B@rMIP [34,35].

The crystal structures of the prepared NH2-MIL-88B, CDs@NH2-MIL-88B and CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP were characterised by XRD. As shown in Fig. S1, the characteristic diffraction peaks of NH2-MIL-88B and CDs@NH2-MIL-88B were consistent with those of the simulated NH2-MIL-88B, which indicated that CDs@NH2-MIL-88B did not change the crystal structure. After modification with MIP, the intensity of characteristic peaks was significantly reduced, indicating the successful synthesis of CDs@NH2-88B@rMIP [35].

Furthermore, the functional groups of CDs, NH2-MIL-88B, CDs@NH2-MIL-88B and CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP were characterised by FT-IR. As shown in Fig. S2, the absorption peaks at 3300 and 1636 cm−1 revealed that the CDs contained O-H and C = O bonds, which endows the CDs with good dispersion in water [33]. In NH2-MIL-88B, the absorption peaks at 3483, 3368 and 1652 cm−1 were derived from the symmetric stretching vibration, asymmetric stretching vibration and bending vibration of N-H on the amino functional group, respectively [37]. The absorption peaks at 1576 and 1380 cm−1 were derived from the stretching vibration of O-H on the carboxyl functional group. The peaks at 1256 and 766 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of C-N and the out-of-plane bending bonding vibration of C-H, respectively [38]. The absorption peaks located at 567 and 518 cm−1 showed Fe-O and Fe-O-Fe on the Fe metal clusters, which confirms that the Fe active site is bonded to the NH2-BDC ligand to form NH2-MIL-88B [39]. In CDs@NH2-MIL-88B, the absorption peaks of the bending vibration of N-H and the stretching vibration of O-H on the NH2-BDC ligand were red-shifted to some extent compared to NH2-MIL-88B. Meanwhile, the peak intensity width were slightly increased, indicating that the ligand successfully restricts CDs into the pore of NH2-MIL-88B by hydrogen bonding [40,41]. The absorption peaks of Si-O-Si and Si-O appeared at 1043 and 457 cm−1 for CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP, which implies successful modification in MIP [42]. The above results demonstrated that the CDs were encapsulated in the pores of NH2-MIL-88B through intermolecular hydrogen bonding and proved the successful preparation of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase-like activity and fluorescent properties of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B
ce:section.ce:para: The peroxidase-like activity of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B was evaluated by UV–visible absorption spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2A, the TMB+H2O2 solution is the blank control (curve a). After adding NH2-MIL-88B, a clear ox-TMB absorption peak appeared at 652 nm, indicating that NH2-MIL-88B has high peroxidase-like activity. Upon the addition of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B, there was a noticeable increase in the absorbance of ox-TMB. This can be attributed to CDs accelerating the electron transfer rate, resulting in CDs@NH2-MIL-88B exhibiting enhanced peroxidase-like activity [43].

The fluorescence properties of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B were investigated using fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 2B). The fluorescence intensity of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B (curve c) was much higher than that of CDs (curve a) and NH2-MIL-88B (curve b) alone. This can be attributed to the improvement in the radiative emission of CDs by restriction of intramolecularly motions (RIM) effect, which enables a higher amount of energy to be redirected back to the ground state through internal transfer, leading to increased fluorescence [44]. Compared to CDs, there was a 20 nm redshift in the fluorescence emission peak of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B. This shift may be attributed to the formation of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between CDs and NH2-MIL-88B, resulting in increased fluorescence of NH2-MIL-88B. These findings suggest that the CDs confined in the pore of NH2-MIL-88B contributed to enhancing peroxidase-like activity and fluorescence intensity. This enhancement is beneficial for developing a highly sensitive CL-FL dual-mode sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of CL-FL dual-mode sensor for OTC detection
ce:section.ce:para: The efficacy of CL mode was initially validated, as shown in Fig. 3A. CDs@NH2-MIL-88B demonstrated a notable peroxidase-like activity, resulting in a more obvious absorption peak of ox-TMB at 652 nm (curve a). Upon adding CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@NIP, a significant decrease in absorbance was observed (curve b), attributed to the lack of imprinted cavities on the surface hindering substrate mass transfer. In contrast, the introduction of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP led to a partial restoration of activity (curve c) due to the presence of molecularly imprinted recognition cavities, which was still lower than that of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B. Interestingly, the absorption peak increased with increasing of OTC concentration (curve d, curve e), which was likely due to the metal complexation reaction of between OTC with Fe3+ in the material, thereby enhancing the electron transfer rate and subsequent peroxidase-like activity [20].

The feasibility of FL mode sensor was then verified (Fig. 3B). Under the excitation of 350 nm, CDs@NH2-MIL-88B has strong fluorescence at 450 nm (curve a). Similar to the CL mode, the fluorescence intensity decreased significantly when CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@NIP was added (curve b). The fluorescence intensity of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP was intermediate between that of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@NIP and CDs@NH2-MIL-88B and increased with the increasing of OTC concentration (curve d, curve e). These results indicated that the constructed CL-FL dual-mode sensor can be used to detect OTC. Meanwhile, the dual signal-on of CL and FL are conducive to further improving the sensitivity of the dual-mode sensor.

The construction and detection process of the CL-FL dual-mode sensor was further validated using zeta potential. As shown in Fig. S3, positively charged -NH2 groups in NH2-MIL-88B result in a zeta potential of +25.0 mV (column a). The zeta potential of the CDs was −25.5 mV (column b). Compared to NH2-MIL-88B, the zeta potential of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B decreased to +9.08 mV (column c), demonstrating that CDs with negative zeta potentials were successfully encapsulated in the pore of NH2-MIL-88B [45]. Due to the molecularly imprinted functional monomer APTES and cross-linker TEOS were enriched with positively charged -NH2 groups. The zeta potential of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP increased to +18.87 mV (column d), which indicates that the molecular imprinting layer is successfully modified on the material surface. After adsorbing 100 nM OTC, the zeta potential of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP was significantly reduced to −2.23 mV (column e), which indicated that the negatively charged OTC molecules were successfully adsorbed by the recognition cavity [42]. The zeta potential further decreased to −6.78 mV as the OTC concentration increased to 1 µM. In summary, the CL-FL dual-mode sensor was successfully constructed and can be used for OTC detection.

To obtain the best analytical performance of the CL-FL sensor, experimental conditions were optimised, including the concentration of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP, recognition time and buffer pH. The difference in absorbance was expressed by ΔA652 (ΔA652= A1-A), in which A and A1 denote the absorbance at 652 nm of the CL mode without or with OTC, respectively. The difference in fluorescence intensity is represented by ΔF450 (ΔF450 = F1-F), in which F and F1 represent the fluorescence intensity of the FL mode at 450 nm, without or with OTC, respectively.

As shown in Fig. S4A, when the concentration of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP was increased from 5 μg mL−1 to 15 μg mL−1, ΔA652 and ΔF450 gradually increased. When the concentration exceeded 15 μg mL−1, ΔA652 and ΔF450 kept decreasing. This was because high concentration might result in CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP stacking and aggregating, which hinders the recognition of OTC by the blotting cavity and thus reduces the CL and FL response signals. Therefore, the optimal concentration of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP was 15 μg mL−1.

To ensure that CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP binds specifically to OTC completely, the OTC recognition time was optimised (Fig. S4B). ΔA652 and ΔF450 gradually increased when the recognition time increased. After 15 min, as CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP bound to OTC completely, ΔA652 and ΔF450 tended to stabilise. Therefore, 15 min was chosen as the optimal recognition time.

Finally, the pH effect of the acetate buffer (NaAc-HAc) was examined. As shown in Fig. S4C, ΔA652 and ΔF450 increased from pH 3.0–4.0. When the pH value exceeded 4.0, ΔA652 and ΔF450 gradually decreased. Therefore, pH = 4.0 was chosen as the optimal pH.

To investigate how OTC enhances the peroxidase-like activity of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP, CV, EIS, XPS, EPR and steady-state enzyme kinetics were examined. CV curves are shown in Fig. 4A, the NH2-MIL-88B presented a pair of redox peaks with a peak potential difference (ΔEp) of 0.258 V (curve a). In contrast, the redox peak current of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B increased and ΔEp decreased to 0.234 V (curve b). This suggests that CDs could effectively increase the electron transfer rate of NH2-MIL-88B, leading to enhanced peroxidase-like activity. After modifying the molecularly imprinted polymer, the redox peak current of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP was significantly decreased and ΔEp was increased to 0.315 V (curve c). This was due to the poorly conductive molecularly imprinted membrane hindering the electron transfer rate. After recognising the OTC, the redox peak current of the CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP increased dramatically, and ΔEp decreased dramatically to 0.161 V (curve d). This could be attributed to the complexation between OTC and Fe3+ on CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP [46], which resulted in an accelerated electron transfer rate and ultimately enhanced peroxidase-like activity.

The mechanism by which OTC complexation accelerated the electron transfer rate of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP was further verified by EIS (Fig. 4B). The data were fitted using the inset Randles equivalent circuit, where Rs, Cdl, Rct and Zw are the electrolyte solution resistance, bilayer capacitance, charge transfer resistance and diffusion resistance, respectively. NH2-MIL-88B has a large impedance with an impedance value of 1437 Ω (curve a). After in situ confining CDs, their impedance value was slightly reduced to 1382 Ω (curve b), which confirms that CDs can effectively accelerate the rate of electron transfer. The CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP had the largest impedance of 1600 Ω (curve c), indicating that molecularly imprinted membranes were successfully modified on the surface of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B. After recognizing OTC, the impedance value decreased dramatically to 458 Ω (curve d), which indicates that OTC dramatically increased the conductivity and accelerated the cycling between Fe3+ and Fe2+, leading to the enhancement of peroxidase-like activity [47].

The valence changes of Fe before and after the addition of OTC were investigated using high-resolution XPS spectra. As shown in Fig. S5, the Fe 2p spectra of NH2-MIL-88B were deconvoluted into five peaks, where the peaks of Fe3+ were located at 728.3 and 713.6 eV, and the corresponding satellite peaks were located at 718.4 eV. The peaks at 725.6 and 711.8 eV represent Fe2+ [48]. The Fe3+ and Fe2+ characteristic peaks of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B are slightly shifted towards the low binding energy, suggesting that CDs provide electrons to NH2-MIL-88B, thereby increasing the electron density around the Fe metal node and enabling its enzyme-like activity [49]. The peaks in CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP are consistent with those of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B. The addition of OTC resulted in a weakening of the intensity of the Fe3+ characteristic peaks and an enhancement of the Fe2+ characteristic peaks, which indicated that OTC was beneficial in accelerating the conversion of Fe3+ to Fe2+. The above results proved that CDs can effectively increase the electron density around Fe and accelerate the electron transfer. Therefore, the acceleration of Fe valence cycling is one of the mechanisms for enhancing peroxidase-like activity.

The radicals in the CL mode were investigated using EPR (Fig. S6A and Fig. S6B). The EPR pattern of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP showed the classical peak patterns of both ·OH and ·O2-, which suggests that they are the main radicals in the reaction system. The peak intensities of ·OH and ·O2- increased dramatically after recognizing OTC, suggesting that OTC could promote the catalysis of H2O2 by CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP to produce more radicals.

The steady-state kinetic measurements were performed to assess peroxidase-like catalytic performance. As shown in Fig. 5A and B, the Km of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP towards TMB and H2O2 were 0.34 mM and 0.39 mM without OTC, respectively. After adding OTC, the Km of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP towards TMB was slightly reduced to 0.21 mM (Fig. 5C), indicating that the affinity of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP for TMB was slightly increased. The Km of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP toward H2O2 was drastically reduced to 0.016 mM (Fig. 5D), indicating that the affinity of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP for H2O2 was drastically increased [50]. Thus, this was the main reason for the enhanced peroxidase-like activity of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP when OTC was present. The above results suggest that the accelerated electron transfer rate and increased affinity for the substrate of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP after recognition of OTC are the main mechanisms by which the CL signals show enhancement.

The FL enhancement mechanism of CDs for NH2-MIL-88B was investigated by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. S7A, NH2-MIL-88B had a broad absorption band of 350–600 nm, largely overlapping with the emission spectrum of CDs. This implies that the FL enhancement of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B may arise from the occurrence of FRET between CDs and NH2-MIL-88B [51]. Since the FL lifetime of receptor molecules is elevated by energy aggregation during FRET [52], the FL quantum yield (FLQY) and FL lifetime changes in NH2-MIL-88B and CDs@NH2-MIL-88B were further investigated. As shown in Fig. S7B and Fig. S7C, the FLQY and FL lifetime of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B were 4.1 % and 16.82 ns, respectively, which were longer than those of NH2-MIL-88B (1.5 % and 15.90 ns). The higher FLQY and longer FL lifetime observed in CDs@NH2-MIL-88B suggest that the increased FL in NH2-MIL-88B was due to FRET from the CDs. In addition, the RIM effect of CDs was confirmed by comparing the FL intensity before and after freezing (Fig. S7D). The weak FL of CDs before freezing is due to the strong vibrational rotation of CDs in the solution, which results in more energy dissipation by non-radiative leaps. After freezing, the vibrational rotation of CDs is suppressed, resulting in the RIM effect, which allows more energy to be returned to the ground state by internal transfer, thus enhancing the FL of CDs. The above results indicate that the mechanism for the enhanced FL of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B was FRET and RIM effect.

To investigate the FL enhancement mechanism of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP induced by OTC, the FLQY and FL lifetime ( τ ) of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP were examined. The FLQY of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP was 3.82 % ( Fig. 6A) with a lifetime of 16.56 ns (Fig. 6C). After adding OTC, the FLQY of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP increased to 5.78 % (Fig. 6B) and also displayed a prolonged lifetime of 17.43 ns (Fig. 6D), which indicated that OTC could enhance the FL of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP. According to the FLQY and FL lifetime, radiative decay rates (k R) and non-radiative decay rates (k nR) of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP with or without OTC were calculated using the following equations [53]:

After adding OTC, the k R of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP increased from 2.31×106 to 3.3×106 S−1, while the K nR decreased from 5.81×107 to 5.41×107 S−1 (Table S1), confirming that RIM effect between OTC and CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP. -OH and -NH2 in OTC may form metal complexes with Fe3+ in CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP, restricting its conformational rotation and ultimately leading to FL enhancement [54]. In summary, the enhanced FL of CDs@NH2-MIL-88B@rMIP was due to the RIM effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of CL-FL dual-mode sensor for OTC detection
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical performance of the CL-FL dual-mode sensor for OTC detection was evaluated under optimal experimental conditions. As shown in Fig. 7A, the absorbance of ox-TMB at 652 nm gradually increased with an increase in the OTC concentration. When the concentration of OTC exceeded 1.00 × 10−6 M, a broad peak appeared at 652 nm, which affected the upper limit of the linear range (Fig. 7B). To obtain a wide linear range, the linearity of CL mode was examined for different reaction times. When the reaction time was reduced to 5 min (Fig. 7C and D) and 3 min (Fig. 7E and F), the linearity of the CL mode was significantly broadened to 1.00 × 10−9 M ∼ 1.00 × 10−5 M (Fig. S8A). Compared with 5 min, the most noticeable colour change was observed at 3 min with the addition of different concentrations of OTC (Fig. S8B). Therefore, the reaction time of 3 min was taken as the best analytical time in the CL mode. At this point, over the range of OTC concentration from 1.00 × 10−9 M to 1.00 × 10−5 M, the linear regression equation was A652 = 0.198 lgCOTC + 2.163 (R2 = 0.995), and the LOD was calculated to be 1.47 × 10−10 M, according to the method recommended by the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) [55].

The analytical performance of the FL mode was examined in Fig. 8A. the FL intensity increased with an increase in the OTC concentration. In the range of OTC concentration from 1.00 × 10−11 M to 1.00 × 10−7 M, F450 showed a good linear relationship with the logarithm of OTC concentration (Fig. 8B), and the linear regression equation was F450 = 1.932 lgCOTC + 25.097 (R2 = 0.996). The LOD was calculated to be 1.84 × 10−12 M according to the method recommended by the IUPAC.

To further investigate the selectivity of the constructed sensor, structural analogues of OTC (TC, CTC), common antibiotics (ROX, ENR, CIP) and eight ions in water (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cl-, SO4 2-, CO3 2- and HCO3 -) were selected as interferences. The concentration of interferents was 10 times the concentration of OTC. As shown in Fig. S9A and Fig. S9C, the A652 of antibiotic and ionic interferents in the CL mode was unchanged, because the interferents cannot be selectively recognised by the molecularly imprinted cavities. Similarly, adding antibiotic interferents and ionic interferents to water did not cause a significant enhancement of F450 (Fig. S9B and Fig. S9D). The above results indicate that the constructed CL-FL sensor has good selectivity and anti-interference, attributed to the selective recognition of OTC by the molecularly imprinted cavity.

The durability and reliability of the CL-FL dual-mode sensor were assessed in the study. As shown in Fig. S10A and Fig. S10B, the CL and FL signals of the sensor maintained 90.03 % and 88.74 % of their original strength following five rounds of reuse, demonstrating the sensor has an excellent reusability. Additionally, Fig. S10C and Fig. S10D display the long-term stability of the CL-FL dual-mode sensor. Due to the stability provided by MIP, the CL and FL signals of the sensor remained at 86.24 % and 90.57 % after 35 days of storage at room temperature, which indicate the sensor has strong long-term stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of OTC in the actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: The actual samples were analysed to assess the practical application of the CL-FL dual mode sensor. Before analysis, the calibration curves of actual samples were established (Fig. S11A and Fig. S11B), which show a reduction slope, possibly due to matrix effects in the water. However, they still show a good linear relationship and can be used for OTC detection. As shown in Table 1, the recoveries of spiked samples in CL mode ranged from 101 % to 112 %, with relative standard deviations (RSD) below 2.56 %. Similarly, recoveries in FL mode were between 101 % and 107 %, with RSD below 3.01 %. These results were consistent with those obtained using LC-MS/MS. Therefore, the CL-FL dual-mode sensor demonstrates reliable detection of OTC in actual samples.

In summary, an innovative CL-FL dual-mode sensing system with dual signal-on based on the molecularly imprinted technique and CDs@NH2-MIL-88B was designed for OTC detection. In this design, CDs was anchored inside NH2-MIL-88B via ship-in-a-bottle, which enhanced the peroxidase-like activity and fluorescence properties. The molecularly imprinted polymer improved the selectivity of the sensor. The unique mechanism of CL-FL dual signal-on was due to the acceleration of electron transfer, increased affinity of the reaction substrate, and FRET, RIM effects caused by OTC recognition. Finaly, the developed sensor exhibited high sensitivity, selectivity, stability and reusability. Meanwhile, the results were consistent with LC-MS/MS standard method for actual water samples detection, affirming the accuracy, reliability and potential application of this dual-mode sensor. This strategy provides a novel path for the construction of high-performance CL-FL dual signal-on sensor platform for environmental pollutant detection.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yuhao Lu is currently studying for M.S. degree under the supervision of Qijian Niu in school of Agricultural Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. His research interest is the fabrication of sensitive and selective molecular imprinting polymers colourimetric and fluorescent sensors for antibiotic detection.

Xiuxiu Dong received her Ph.D. degree in biomedical engineering from Southeast University (SEU) in 2020. Currently, she is working in School of Agricultural Engineering, Jiangsu University as an associate professor. Her research interests include the rational design of functional nanomaterials and the construction of nanobiosensors (e.g., photoelectrochemical sensor, electrochemical sensor, fluorescence sensor), which are applied in tumor marker detection, food safety analysis, environmental monitoring and so on.

Guanya Ji received his Ph.D. degree in China Agricultural University (CAU) in 2018. He is now an associate professor at School of Agricultural Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. His research cover high-value utilization technologies for agricultural biomass, and prevention of agricultural source pollution.

Xuejing Wang is currently studying for M.S. degree under the supervision of Qijian Niu in school of Agricultural Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. Her research interest is the fabrication of sensitive and selective molecular imprinting polymers photoelectrochemical sensors for persistent organic pollutant detection.

Tao Wang is currently studying for M.S. degree under the supervision of Qijian Niu in school of Agricultural Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. His research interest is the fabrication of sensitive and selective molecular imprinting polymers photoelectrochemical sensors for antibiotic detection.

Chengshun Tang is currently studying for M.S. degree under the supervision of Qijian Niu in school of Agricultural Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. His research interest is the fabrication of porous functional material for persistent organic pollutant adsorption and degradation.

Oluwafunmilola Ola is an Assistant Professor of Materials Engineering at University of Nottingham. Her research is focused on the development of advanced materials and nanocomposites for energy and sensor related applications. Her research was recognized by panels on both successful Leverhulme Early Career and Nottingham Research Fellowships, resulting in 7 research grants as principal investigator for around £1.1 M. Her research has led to over 51 publications in peer-reviewed journals, 3 book chapters and 38 international conferences largely recognised by the international community.

Hongwei Duan received his Ph.D. degree in China Agricultural University (CAU) in 2020. He is now an associate professor at College of Mechanical and Electrical Engineering, Shihezi University, China. His research cover spectral imaging, computer vision, electrochemistry and their applications in agricultural sensing.

Qian Liu is an associate professor at School of Agricultural Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. She obtained her Ph.D. in Processing and Storage of Agriculture Products from Jiangsu University, China in 2014. Her current research interests include the quality and safety detection of agricultural products based on photoelectrochemical sensing technology.

Qijian Niu received his Ph.D. degree in Beijing University of Chemical Technology (BUCT) in 2018. He is now an associate professor at School of Agricultural Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. His research cover photoelectrochemical, electrochemistry, novel nanomaterials and their applications in agricultural sensing.
